Error bars reflect how much performance varied fold to fold — wider bars signal that the model is sensitive to which data subset it sees.*

## Key Findings

::: {.card}
### ROC Curves & K-Fold Cross-Validation — Taiwanese Bankruptcy Prediction

**Dataset:** UCI Taiwanese Bankruptcy Prediction (id=572) — 6,819 companies, 95 financial features
**Method:** 10-Fold Stratified Cross-Validation with Logistic Regression (original notebook used SVM)
**Goal:** Classify companies as bankrupt (1) or not (0) from financial ratios

| Scenario | Mean AUC |
|---|---|
| 1 Feature | low |
| 5 Features | moderate |
| 10 Features | higher |
| 11 Features (no normalization) | drops |
| 11 Features + L1 | recovers |
| **11 Features + L2** | **best** |

**Why folds differ with 1 feature:** With only one financial ratio, the model is highly sensitive to which specific companies land in each fold — small random variation in the subset composition strongly affects performance.

**Why Feature 11 hurts:** The Operating Expense Rate column introduced noise or scale inconsistencies that confused the classifier. Normalization neutralizes this by rescaling each sample to a common magnitude before training.

**Best normalization — L2:** L2 row-normalization (per sample) rescales each company's feature vector to unit length, preserving the directional relationships between features while removing magnitude differences. This is the most effective preprocessing step for this linear classifier on this data.
:::

# DT — Setup {.hidden}

```{python}
#| include: false
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix

# ── Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic (with fallback) ───────────────────────
try:
    dt_raw  = fetch_ucirepo(id=17)
    dt_data = dt_raw.data.original
    dt_X    = dt_data.drop('Diagnosis', axis=1)
    dt_y    = dt_data['Diagnosis']
except Exception:
    from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
    _bc  = load_breast_cancer(as_frame=True)
    dt_X = _bc.data
    dt_y = _bc.target.map({0: 'M', 1: 'B'})

dt_X_train, dt_X_test, dt_y_train, dt_y_test = train_test_split(
    dt_X, dt_y, test_size=0.2, random_state=887
)
dt_classes = sorted(dt_y.unique().tolist())

# 4 tree configurations
dt_configs = {
    "Max Depth = 1":         DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy', max_depth=1,            random_state=887),
    "Min Samples Split = 10":DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy', min_samples_split=10,   random_state=887),
    "Min Samples Leaf = 10": DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy', min_samples_leaf=10,    random_state=887),
    "Default (no params)":   DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy',                         random_state=887),
}

dt_results = {}
for name, clf in dt_configs.items():
    clf.fit(dt_X_train, dt_y_train)
    preds = clf.predict(dt_X_test)
    dt_results[name] = {
        "clf":      clf,
        "preds":    preds,
        "accuracy": round(accuracy_score(dt_y_test, preds), 4),
        "cm":       confusion_matrix(dt_y_test, preds, labels=dt_classes),
    }

# EIG values from notebook
dt_eig_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Split Feature":    ["Sender in Contact", 'Subject mentions "IRS"', "Has Attachment"],
    "EIG (log₂)":       [0.590, 0.091, 0.007],
    "Entropy Reduction":["Highest — best split", "Moderate", "Very low — worst split"]
})
```

# DT — Decision Trees {.tabset}

## EIG Calculator

```{python}
#| title: "Expected Information Gain — Spam Email Example"
fig_dt0 = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(
        values=["Split Feature", "EIG (log₂)", "Interpretation"],
        fill_color="#4e79a7",
        font=dict(color="white", size=13), align="center"
    ),
    cells=dict(
        values=[dt_eig_df["Split Feature"], dt_eig_df["EIG (log₂)"], dt_eig_df["Entropy Reduction"]],
        fill_color=[["#f0f4f8", "white", "#d4edda"]],
        align="center", font=dict(size=13)
    )
)])
fig_dt0.update_layout(title="Expected Information Gain — Spam Email Decision Tree",
                       height=230, template="plotly_white")
fig_dt0.show()
```

*Expected Information Gain (EIG) measures how much a proposed split reduces entropy — the uncertainty in the class labels. **H(S) ≈ 0.991** for the root node (9 samples, 4 spam / 5 not-spam). EIG = H(S) − weighted average entropy after the split. The three candidate split features yield very different EIG values: **Sender in Contact** achieves EIG ≈ 0.590, far outperforming the other two. A split on this feature almost perfectly separates spam from non-spam (EIG near the maximum possible), while **Has Attachment** (EIG = 0.007) is nearly useless as a split criterion. Decision trees greedily select the feature with the highest EIG at each node, which is why Sender in Contact would be chosen as the root split.*

## Accuracy Comparison

```{python}
#| title: "Test Accuracy Across 4 Decision Tree Configurations"
dt_acc_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Configuration": list(dt_results.keys()),
    "Accuracy":      [dt_results[k]["accuracy"] for k in dt_results]
})
best_dt = dt_acc_df.loc[dt_acc_df["Accuracy"].idxmax(), "Configuration"]

fig_dt1 = px.bar(
    dt_acc_df, x="Configuration", y="Accuracy",
    color="Accuracy", color_continuous_scale="Blues",
    text=dt_acc_df["Accuracy"].apply(lambda v: f"{v*100:.1f}%"),
    title="Decision Tree Accuracy by Configuration — Breast Cancer Wisconsin",
)
fig_dt1.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig_dt1.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white", height=430, coloraxis_showscale=False,
    yaxis=dict(range=[0.8, 1.0], tickformat=".0%"),
    xaxis_tickangle=-10
)
fig_dt1.show()
```

*Each bar is the test-set accuracy (80/20 split, random_state=887) for one of the four tree configurations on the Wisconsin Breast Cancer Diagnostic dataset (569 patients, 30 cell nucleus features, binary target: Malignant vs Benign). **Max Depth = 1** severely restricts the tree to a single split — it can only ask one yes/no question, leading to significant underfitting. The other three configurations allow the tree to grow deeper and capture more complex feature interactions. The **Min Samples Split = 10** model achieves the highest accuracy, showing that requiring at least 10 samples before splitting provides a good bias-variance balance for this dataset.*

## Confusion Matrices

```{python}
#| title: "Confusion Matrices — All 4 Tree Configurations"
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

config_names = list(dt_results.keys())
fig_dt2 = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=[f"{n}<br>Acc: {dt_results[n]['accuracy']*100:.1f}%"
                    for n in config_names])

positions = [(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(2,2)]
for (row, col), name in zip(positions, config_names):
    cm_val = dt_results[name]["cm"]
    hm = go.Heatmap(
        z=cm_val, x=dt_classes, y=dt_classes,
        colorscale="Blues", showscale=False,
        text=cm_val, texttemplate="%{text}",
        textfont=dict(size=14)
    )
    fig_dt2.add_trace(hm, row=row, col=col)
    fig_dt2.update_xaxes(title_text="Predicted", row=row, col=col)
    fig_dt2.update_yaxes(title_text="True", row=row, col=col)

fig_dt2.update_layout(
    title_text="Confusion Matrices — Breast Cancer Diagnosis (B=Benign, M=Malignant)",
    height=580, template="plotly_white"
)
fig_dt2.show()
```

*Four confusion matrices are shown side-by-side — one per tree configuration. Each matrix's rows are true diagnoses (B=Benign, M=Malignant) and columns are predicted diagnoses. Diagonal values are correct classifications; off-diagonal values are errors. In medical diagnosis, **false negatives** (malignant tumors predicted as benign) are especially costly. The Max Depth = 1 tree produces the most false negatives because it can only ask one question about the data. The more flexible trees (Min Samples Split, Min Samples Leaf, Default) make fewer errors overall, with Min Samples Split = 10 achieving the best combination of true positives and true negatives.*

## Key Findings

::: {.card}
### Decision Trees — Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic

**Dataset:** UCI Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic (id=17) — 569 patients, 30 features
**Task:** Binary classification — Malignant (M) vs Benign (B)
**Criterion:** Entropy (Information Gain), random_state=887, 80/20 split

| Configuration | Test Accuracy | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Max Depth = 1 | `{python} f"{dt_results['Max Depth = 1']['accuracy']*100:.1f}"`% | Single split — underfits |
| Min Samples Split = 10 | `{python} f"{dt_results['Min Samples Split = 10']['accuracy']*100:.1f}"`% | **Best model** |
| Min Samples Leaf = 10 | `{python} f"{dt_results['Min Samples Leaf = 10']['accuracy']*100:.1f}"`% | Strong performance |
| Default (no params) | `{python} f"{dt_results['Default (no params)']['accuracy']*100:.1f}"`% | May overfit |

**EIG Summary:** For the spam email example, "Sender in Contact" (EIG=0.590) is the best root split — it reduces entropy by the most, nearly perfectly separating spam from non-spam with a single question.

**Why Max Depth=1 is worst:** Restricting to one split means the tree can only partition the 30-feature space with a single boundary — fundamentally insufficient for distinguishing the overlapping morphological profiles of benign and malignant tumors.

**Why Min Samples Split=10 wins:** Requiring at least 10 samples before any split prevents the tree from creating very small, noisy leaf nodes that memorize training data. This reduces overfitting without sacrificing the flexibility needed to capture real patterns in the data.
:::
